medications.drug_name: any oral analgesic or anti-inflammatory as sole home management
medications.indication_rationale: Home analgesia without urgent in-person assessment risks masking the evolving clinical picture of a likely infected gallbladder (and possible cholangitis) and delays antibiotics and definitive treatment.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected acute cholecystitis/possible cholangitis requiring urgent hospital assessment

medications.indication_rationale: Strict nil-by-mouth is appropriate pending assessment given ongoing vomiting and the possibility of urgent intervention (e.g. cholecystectomy or ERCP).
contraindications_in_this_case: Possible need for urgent procedural intervention

medications.indication_rationale: No indication to stop the patient's regular statin while arranging urgent assessment; this can be reviewed by the treating hospital team.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and avoid unnecessary movement while arranging transport to the Emergency Department.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minimises discomfort while urgent assessment is arranged.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not eat or drink anything further; go to the Emergency Department today, not tomorrow.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces risk if urgent procedural intervention is required and avoids delay in treating a likely infected gallbladder.

patient_education_points.point: Your pain lasting far longer than your usual episodes, together with the fever and chills, suggests your gallbladder may now be infected rather than just having a stone temporarily block it — and the possible yellow tinge to your eyes means we also need to check whether the blockage has affected your bile duct.
Please go to the Emergency Department today — this needs blood tests, a scan, and likely antibiotics, and possibly a procedure to relieve any blockage.
Please don't eat or drink anything else until you've been assessed.
If the pain spreads to your whole abdomen, you become confused, or you feel like you might faint, that's more urgent still — call 000 immediately if that happens.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by urgent ED referral — follow-up arranged by the emergency, surgical, and/or gastroenterology team
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: results of bloods/imaging, antibiotic course completion, elective or interval cholecystectomy planning, GP medication review
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading/generalised abdominal pain, confusion, feeling faint, or worsening jaundice — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Explicit statement that this is likely an infected gallbladder (cholecystitis) and possibly involves the bile duct (cholangitis)
Safety-netting for worsening/generalised pain or confusion (possible sepsis/perforation)
acceptable_alternatives: Calling an ambulance instead of driving is an acceptable alternative, particularly if the patient's condition appears to be worsening
errors_of_omission: No mention of possible cholangitis given the jaundice history
errors_of_commission: Recommending home analgesia/anti-inflammatories and watchful waiting as primary management
Treating this as a recurrence of ordinary biliary colic without escalation
Recommending routine or next-week GP follow-up instead of same-day ED referral
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Any response that does not include both (1) urgent same-day ED referral AND (2) an explicit statement that this may be an infected gallbladder requiring hospital treatment is a significant failure on the management domain given the fever, persistent pain, and possible jaundice.
